item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nukes?
To suppress the film industry?

I'm sorry, but this is just… really dumb. I don't even know what criticism to give, the core concept itself is just so boring. This article, at least to me, is really bland. -1

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: This might be a little late, but would Protocol Blackout perhaps be linkable to the similarly named, similarly-purposed Operation Blackout in SCP-2998-5?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: This is fine.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. The author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright. So, in addition to an in-line link at the mention of the GOC to their hub page, another possible link could be made between here any really any film based anomaly. Of particular note is SCP-3084 since it highlights that not only the US film industry is causing problems, and it may infact be motion pictures in general that are anomalous. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Mutual sharing of detection statistics with the GOC has been authorized for the purposes of improving detection rates. Detection statistics for the prior month should be exchanged at the end of each month. A special liaison position has been created for this purpose.

To this:

Mutual sharing of detection statistics with the GOC has been authorized for the purposes of improving detection rates. Detection statistics for the prior month should be exchanged at the end of each month. A special liaison position has been created for this purpose.

Changing this:

Similar phenomena have been observed in other regional film industries, in particular the Indian and European industries, which have been given their own designations. For a detailed analysis of these phenomena, see: Jacobson, Suzanne. "An Analysis of Modern Mass Media as Massive Cognitohazard Generators" Fieldwork: An SCP Foundation Journal (2008): 601.

To this:

Similar phenomena have been observed in other regional film industries, in particular the Indian and European industries, which have been given their own designations. For a detailed analysis of these phenomena, see: Jacobson, Suzanne. "An Analysis of Modern Mass Media as Massive Cognitohazard Generators" Fieldwork: An SCP Foundation Journal (2008): 601.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is super interesting, but the logs seemed kinda underwhelming. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep- check out addendum 3.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does Bollywood generate cognitohazards?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Nyka, your comment re: this article is vague. While you are free to express your satisfaction or dissatisfaction towards a given article, it is not helping the author in any way. If you state that it is a "wasted" opportunity, do elaborate and provide information to your statement. While you do not need to be in-depth, the current statement hardly counts as meaningful critique of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gee, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the biggest wasted opportunities on the site, in my honest opinion. With an environment as vast and varied as the Hollywood film industry, and an idea of it being an inadvertent creator of cognitohazards, it's squandered by the effects in question being predictable and dull, with little payoff in the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I meant the mainlist title. It was changed at some point in the past.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't a rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the original title better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I gotta agree with augmentedspartan. Plus, mass Foundation coverups for huge things like this always completely break suspension for me. The only reason Star Signals did it well is because it went in detail on how it was performed, how difficult it was, how it went wrong, and how it was more luck than skill.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure if this is necro, but thriving the call for an extended test log. Maybe it could bring more attention to the SCP and revive it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this SCP a lot, but I agree with you quite a lot too and I was wondering if something like the Rambo movie being one of these SCPs and the viewer starts to fight all non american people with imaginary guns and yelling in their best Rambo impersonation or something like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's possible that the anomalies would have different effects in a different context, but do what they do because it's associated with the movies that they happen to be in. Just a thought.

item.title: Re: MPAA=SCP?
item.content:encoded: That would be interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm inclined to agree with this. Since these cognitohazards are produced unintentionally, it makes little sense for them to line up so closely with the films.

Still good enough for an upvote from me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nthing the call for an extended log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really think this could use an extended log.

item.title: Re: MPAA=SCP?
item.content:encoded: This is exactly what I thought of when I read it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think what this article is lacking is any exploration as to the causes of these anomalies. The initial impression that I got was that Hollywood pumps out so much information that it just inevitably forms a few cognitohazards, however the test logs suggest that they are purposefully put into the films (or why would the effects be related to the films at all?)

I don't think the North American film industry is actually the skip here, I think it's the anomalies that are the skips or possibly the group(s) responsible for putting them in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like you misunderstood the examples. With the exception of the last one, all the cognitohazards were caught during production and removed by the Foundation. So the films that people saw didn't have the cognitohazards in them.

The obvious exception to this is the last one, which did get released with the cognitohazard in it. The Foundation response (releasing an inoculation meme) neutralizes it however, effectively removing it from the film.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this stretches believability beyond the breaking point. So many people have seen those movies in real life and nothing has happened, I just can't buy this at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only really novel or intriguing thing that this has going for it is scale. The hazards that you enumerate are pretty simplistic and not that interesting by themselves. Scale can work as a hook, but here it wasn't enough to overcome how little the actual effects interested me.

item.title: Re: MPAA=SCP?
item.content:encoded: In some different versions of this, I did have the MPAA being used as another means of containment, but it was hard to work it into the final skip. Also, in regards to your second question… well, they are. Of the three cognitohazard examples, two get caught while in production, and the last one avoids detection because it's delayed-response.

item.title: MPAA=SCP?
item.content:encoded:
I was hoping you would touch on the Motion Picture Association film rating system as a covert means to monitor. It would be interesting to figure why indie films get little assistance with adapting their film to the MPAA standard, while the big Hollywood films get bullet points on what they ought to change to get the desirable rating (usually PG-13).

Also, so many people pre-screen these films…why do the hundreds of people who edit/render/monitor for marketing ideas/test-screen, not be affected?

In a past life, I was in the film/television industry. Some ideas are percolating around this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The problem isn't that the effect are behavioral, but that they are too obvious and too tied to the movies themselves. I can see that you're trying to make the effects be relevant to the movie, but maybe you should do something more subtle.

Personally I would prefer something that is just weird. Not necessarily random, but maybe have effects that at first glance seem to be arbitrary to the movie, but that in reality have some sense of hidden logic or connectivity, something that isn't too obvious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea, and I honestly think there's only a bit more work that needs to be put into this for it to really work. I think you should check out some SCPs like the yellowstone anomaly for some inspiration on how to really finish it. +1 for potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the response. Letting an author know why you're downvoting something is probably one of the best things you can do.

In regards to your issues with the article, I originally had a set of three unnamed films that had effects that weren't behavioral altering at all. However, based on the responses I got to that (which were basically "make it more interesting") I changed up the examples entirely and tried to be a bit more creative than just "thing that gives you nausea" "thing that causes internal bleeding" etc… Apparently, I either went too far in this or not far enough.

Do you feel it would be improved if I made one of them a non-behavioral altering one? Say, make the Toy Story one actually turn people into toys? (Just as a hypothetical example.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

At first I thought it was dumb, then I thought about it for a little bit. Yes, OF COURSE the largest producer of mass media would accidentally produces cognitohazards in a world where cognitozards exist!

For a little bit, I thought this was too "omnipresent Foundation" (like the color one, which I don't like), but no, I think this stays just on the right side of the line of believable logistics for the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hollywood as anomalous… a rather ambitious task at hand. It is interesting that Hollywood becomes a hotbed of anomalous activities. However, I find the various effects to be underwhelming. They seem random and arbitrary, and could well be separate SCPs with Hollywood as a GoI.

I will have to downvote since I don't feel the effect meets up to the scale of "Hollywood SCP".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reading this comment, I have to agree with you. However, it is not enough to keep me from upvoting, but I would recommend that the author consider your advice nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a weird read for me. Not because of the SCP itself, but because while reading I kept changing between "this sounds interesting" and "oh this sounds dumb". Sadly it ended with the latter. The idea of there being small cognitohazards in Hollywood movies sounded interesting, but the effects themselves were really boring. The movie with living toys makes people believe they are toys. The movie about migratory birds makes people want to migrate. And so on. The effects were really easy to guess after the first one and were really dull, which is not a good thing since the SCP doesn't have anything else going for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know why, but I really, really like this

+1

EDIT: I'm pretty sure these have fallen out of style by now, but an extended test log would be pretty cool (regularly pruned for quality, of course). There's a lot of fun places this idea could be taken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an idea that I had several months ago. At the time, I considered it an incredibly dumb idea. Maybe it still is. Hopefully, it's a decent skip.

Edit: Thanks to (in no particular order) TwistedGears, Decibelle, TyGently, minmin, Randomini, Wogglebug, EldritchCyanide, and eveyone else who helped review this as a draft. Give me a poke if I forgot your name.
